You run a company and you’ve doing well. Now you want to take some money out of your company for yourself. Hooray! But before you rush off to do it, have a think about how you want to pay yourself, because doing it the wrong way can trigger big tax headaches. Here’s a quick guide to doing it right.

Quick Summary

Taking money out of your company is to be expected - how else are you going to get paid? But how you do it matters.

• If you treat it like salary – it’s simple but you need payroll systems.

• If you treat it like a dividend -  you need profit, the correct share class, and formal declaration.

• If you treat it like a loan - you need an agreement, payment terms, interest and repayment schedules. You also need to be aware of the timeline.

Why this matters

Even if you own the company, your company’s money isn’t automatically your money.

Think of your company as a separate person with its own bank account. When you take money out of it, the ATO (that’s the Australian Taxation Office) wants to know why - is it:

• a wage or salary

• a dividend (profit distribution)

• a loan you’ll pay back later?

If you just transfer money out without recording what it’s for, the ATO will probably assume you’ve taken company profits for yourself - like paying yourself a bonus - and they’ll tax you on it.

A quick look at tax credits

Before we talk about how you pay yourself from a company, it helps to understand how tax credits work in Australia—especially franking system and Division 7A, which is a set of rules designed to stop company owners from taking money out of the business tax-free.

A company pays tax at the 25% corporate tax rate. But when those profits are later paid to you as a shareholder, they’re treated as dividends and taxed at your personal tax rate (which can be as high as 45% plus medicare levy 2%).

This is where the franking system comes in.

What is the franking system?

Australia uses a system called imputation, which simply means this:
the ATO doesn’t want company profits to be taxed twice—once in the company’s hands and again in yours.

So if a company has already paid tax on its profits, it can pass that tax on to shareholders as franking credits.
When you receive a franked dividend, you also receive those credits, which you can use to reduce your own tax bill.

If you receive an unfranked dividend, it means the company hasn’t paid tax on that profit. In that case, you pay tax on the full amount at your personal marginal rate.

Why this matters

If you take money out of your company the wrong way, the ATO can treat it as a “deemed dividend” under Div7A rules. Typically they don’t come with franking credits - so they end up being fully taxable.

This means:

• The money you take from your company will be added to your personal income, and

• You may end up paying more tax than if it had been set up properly.

What are your main options for putting money in your pocket?

Below are the three common ways to take money out - with easy pros and cons.

Option 1 - Pay yourself a salary or wage (through payroll/STP)

Pros

• Straightforward: you’re treated like an employee.

• Tax is withheld and reported automatically via STP/BAS.

• Superannuation (SG) is handled as part of payroll.

• Great for stable, regular income.

Cons

• You need STP-enabled payroll software.

• The company must withhold PAYG tax and pay super.

• Ongoing admin (or outsource to your accountant).

Best for when:

you’re working in the business and want regular pay, or

the company can manage ongoing payroll and super payments.

Option 2 - Declare a dividend (shareholder distribution)

Pros

• If the company has paid tax, dividends can carry franking credits to reduce your personal tax.

• Good for one-off profit distributions.

• Useful when your company has accumulated franking credits you want to use.

Cons

• You need distributable profits to pay dividends.

• You must treat all shareholders of the same share class equally.

• Requires documentation (dividend statements, minutes) and correct tax reporting.

Best for when:

The company has clear profits and you want to share those profits with shareholders rather than create a loan.

The company has unused franking credits and you want to avoid extra tax on money the company already paid tax on.

An example: you issued yourself $10,000 dividend

If the company already paid $2,500 tax on the profit, you receive a $10,000 dividend with a $2,500 franking credit. You declare $12,500 income but subtract the $2,500 credit from your personal tax bill - preventing double taxation.

⚠️Tax Warning!

Franking credit deficit

If the company gives out too many franking credits, it may need to pay extra tax to top up the franking account.

Franking credit trap

If the corporate tax rate falls (e.g., 27.5% → 25%), some credits from earlier years may become “stuck” because you can only frank dividends at the current lower rate.

Planning ahead helps avoid accidentally wasting credits.

To manage this, consider:

• Paying dividends earlier (before a rate drop takes effect) to use up higher-rate credits.

• Also, review your personal tax position — if you’re on a lower marginal tax rate, you may receive a refund of franking credits. If your personal tax rate is high, you may prefer that the company retain profits until it can pay fully franked dividends at the higher rate OR until other strategies can unlock trapped credits.

• Looking ahead, it’s worth planning for future profits to make sure trapped credits can be used later and checking your company’s base rate entity status each year, as this affects the maximum franking rate you can apply. A base rate entity is generally a company with turnover under $50 million and where no more than 80% of its income is from passive sources such as rent, interest, or dividends.

You can find more information about franking credits on the ATO website.

Option 3 - Take money as a Division 7A loan (formal director loan)

This is when the company “lends” you money. It’s fine — as long as you follow the rules.

Pros

• Immediate cashflow without payroll and PAYG withholding.

• No immediate tax if the loan is compliant.

• You get extra time before the first minimum yearly repayment (MYR).

Cons

• Requires a written loan agreement by the company’s lodgment day.

• Needs minimum yearly repayments (principal + interest).

• Interest must be charged at/above the ATO benchmark rate (8.37% for 2025–26).

• If you break the rules, the loan becomes a deemed unfranked dividend — and fully taxable.

Best for when:

• You need cash now and the company (and you) are confident you can meet the repayment schedule.

• You want to avoid payroll and super in the short term but can commit to formalising the loan and making minimum yearly repayments.

• You are okay with more documentation / formal structure, and are comfortable with the idea of it being a loan (not a “free withdrawal”).

title: Sole Trader or Company? How to Choose the Right Structure
title_zh: 个体工商户与公司——您应该选择哪一个？
author: Livia Lin, CA CTA
excerpt: Starting a business can be exciting but confusing. in this article explains the key differences between a sole trader and a company in simple terms, so you may choose the right structure for your goals.
excerpt_zh: 创业可能令人兴奋，但也令人困惑。本指南用简单的英语解释了个体工商户和公司之间的主要区别，以便您可以选择适合您目标的结构。
content_text:
Editor note: The following article has been adapted for Flexpath readers while preserving the same technical meaning.

Starting a business is exciting, but structure decisions can be tricky. This article explains the key differences between a sole trader and a company in plain English, so you may choose the right structure for your goals.

Starting a business in Australia? One of the biggest decisions you’ll make early on is how to set it up. Should you keep things simple and go it alone as a sole trader, or register a company and give your business its own legal life?

Both options can work perfectly - it really depends on your goals, how much you’re earning, and how much risk you’re willing to take. Get it right from the start, and you’ll save yourself a lot of time, stress, and tax surprises later on.

Let’s break it down in plain English.

What’s a Sole Trader?

Being a sole trader means it’s just you - you run the show, keep the profits, and make all the decisions. It’s quick and affordable to set up, and perfect for freelancers, small side hustles, or anyone testing a business idea.

The flip side? You and the business are legally the same. So if your business owes money or gets into trouble, your personal assets (like your home or car) could be on the line. You’ll also report all business income and expenses on your personal tax return.

Best for:

• Solo operators and freelancers

• Low-cost, low-risk businesses

• Anyone starting out or testing an idea

What’s a Company?

A company is a separate legal entity. It’s like a totally different person in the eyes of the law. It has its own ABN, ACN, and TFN. That means it can earn money, own assets, sign contracts, and take on debts in its own name.

You (and any co-owners) become shareholders, and the company directors make the day-to-day decisions. The company files its own tax return and pays tax at the company rate — 25% for small businesses in 2025.

Because it’s separate, your personal liability is limited — so your house and savings are generally protected if something goes wrong.

Best for:

• Businesses planning to grow or hire staff

• Those wanting to attract investors or raise capital

• Companies holding valuable assets or operating in higher-risk industries

When Does It Make Sense to Switch from Sole Trader to Company?

A lot of small businesses start out as sole traders - it’s simple, flexible, easy to run. But as profits grow, a company structure often makes more sense.

You might think about switching if:

• Your taxable income consistently hits $130k–$150k+

• You want to protect your personal assets

• You plan to reinvest profits or bring in investors

• You’re hiring more employees or moving into a higher-risk industry

Meet Emma – A Real Life Example

Emma runs a catering business in Sydney.

As a sole trader:

• She earned about $140,000 profit in the 2025 financial year

• Paid tax at her personal rate (37% + Medicare levy)

• Worked mostly with contractors

• Kept things simple with GST on a cash basis

As her business grew, so did her profits (and her tax bill). She realised she could keep more of her earnings if her business paid the 25% company tax rate instead of her personal rate. She also wanted to hire permanent staff and protect her personal assets.

So, she made the switch.

*Rounded, simplified figures for example purposes

By forming a company, Emma could reinvest more of her profit, hire employees, and build credibility with clients and investors.

What Actually Changes When You Form a Company

• New business identity: register the company, then set up ABN/ACN/TFN and a dedicated business bank account.

• Hiring more staff: Run payroll, make PAYG payments, report GST and PAYGW in your BAS.

• Company tax: Lodge a company tax return and pay 25% tax on profits.

• Paying yourself: Take money out as wages (taxed as salary) or dividends (with franking credits).

• Asset protection: Your personal assets are safer — though directors still have some responsibilities.

• Growth & investment: Easier to raise capital and attract investors.

• Branding boost: “Pty Ltd” adds credibility to your business name.

What About Transferring Assets (like a Car)?

Emma’s car is a depreciating asset, so it loses value over time. When she moves it from her sole trader business into her company, it triggers a 'balancing adjustment event'. In simple terms, any gain or loss from the car affects her personal taxable income.

That means any gain or loss may affect her personal taxable income - but there’s a possible small business restructure rollover (Subdivision 328‑G) that can help her avoid immediate tax, if she meet certain conditions.

In practice, most business cars lose value, so any gain or loss is usually small, and rollovers rarely provide a significant tax benefit.

Emma will also need to transfer the registration and pay stamp duty, so the company becomes the legal owner.

Before You Decide – Ask Yourself

• How much profit do I expect in the next few years?

• How much risk am I comfortable taking personally?

• Will I reinvest profits or distribute them?

• Am I planning to hire employees or attract investors?

• Do I need asset protection or extra credibility?

• Am I okay with more paperwork for better long-term benefits?

A Quick Note on Personal Services Income (PSI)

If most of your income comes from your personal skills or labour - like consulting or freelancing - PSI rules might apply. They can affect how you claim deductions and whether income-splitting through a company is allowed. We’ve got a separate blog explaining PSI in plain English.

The Bottom Line

Sole trader: simple, flexible, and low-cost - great for starting out.
Company: structured, protective, and scalable - great for growing.

Many Australian businesses begin as sole traders and move to a company when it’s time to level up.

Key takeaway: choose the structure that matches your goals, income, and risk profile, and confirm the setup with your accountant before you proceed.

Got questions? Reach out to us via app chat. You can also find more info about business structure on this website.
content_text_zh:
创业可能令人兴奋，但也令人困惑。本指南用简单的英语解释了个体工商户和公司之间的主要区别，以便您可以选择适合您目标的结构。

在澳大利亚创业？您早期要做的最大决定之一就是如何设置它。您应该让事情变得简单并作为个体经营者单独经营，还是注册一家公司并赋予您的企业自己的合法生命？

这两种选择都可以完美发挥作用——这实际上取决于您的目标、您的收入以及您愿意承担多少风险。从一开始就做好，您将为自己节省大量时间、压力和以后的税务意外。

让我们用简单的英语来分解它。

什么是个体工商户？

作为一名个体经营者意味着只有你一个人——你掌控一切，保留利润，并做出所有决定。它的设置速度快且价格实惠，非常适合自由职业者、小型副业或任何测试商业想法的人。

另一方面？您和企业在法律上是相同的。因此，如果您的企业欠钱或陷入麻烦，您的个人资产（例如您的房屋或汽车）可能会面临风险。您还将在个人纳税申报表上报告所有业务收入和支出。

最适合：

• 个体经营者和自由职业者

• 低成本、低风险业务

• 任何开始或测试某个想法的人

什么是公司？

公司是一个独立的法人实体。在法律眼中，这就像一个完全不同的人。它有自己的 ABN、ACN 和 TFN。这意味着它可以以自己的名义赚钱、拥有资产、签订合同并承担债务。

您（和任何共同所有者）成为股东，公司董事做出日常决策。该公司自行提交纳税申报表并按公司税率纳税——到 2025 年，小型企业的税率为 25%。

因为它是独立的，所以你的个人责任是有限的——所以如果出现问题，你的房子和储蓄通常会受到保护。

最适合：

• 计划增加或雇用员工的企业

• 那些想要吸引投资者或筹集资金的人

• 持有有价值资产或从事高风险行业的公司

什么时候从个体经营者转向公司有意义？

许多小企业都是从个体经营者开始的——它简单、灵活、易于运营。但随着利润的增长，公司结构往往更有意义。

如果出现以下情况，您可能会考虑切换：

• 您的应税收入持续达到 $130k–$150k+

• 您想要保护您的个人资产

• 您计划将利润再投资或引入投资者

• 您正在雇用更多员工或进入风险较高的行业

认识艾玛——现实生活中的例子

艾玛在悉尼经营一家餐饮企业。

作为个体工商户：

• 她在 2025 财年赚取了约 140,000 美元的利润

• 按她的个人税率缴税（37% + 医疗保险税）

• 主要与承包商合作

• 以现金收付制的商品及服务税让一切变得简单

随着她的业务增长，她的利润（以及税单）也随之增长。她意识到，如果她的企业缴纳 25% 的公司税率而不是她的个人税率，她可以保留更多收入。她还想雇用长期员工并保护她的个人资产。

所以，她做出了转变。

*四舍五入的简化数字仅供示例之用

通过成立一家公司，艾玛可以将更多利润进行再投资、雇用员工并在客户和投资者中建立信誉。

当你成立公司时，实际会发生什么变化

• 新的企业身份：完成公司注册后，依次办理 ABN/ACN/TFN，并开立独立对公账户。

• 雇用更多员工：管理工资单、进行现收现付付款、在您的 BAS 中报告 GST 和 PAYGW。

• 公司税：提交公司纳税申报表并缴纳25% 的利润税。

• 支付自己费用：将钱取出作为工资（按工资征税）或股息（含红利抵免）。

• 资产保护：您的个人资产更加安全——尽管董事仍然负有一些责任。

• 增长和投资：更容易筹集资金和吸引投资者。

• 品牌提升：“Pty Ltd”增加了您企业名称的可信度。

转移资产（如汽车）怎么样？

艾玛的汽车是一种会贬值的资产，因此随着时间的推移它会失去价值。当她将其从个体贸易商业务转移到她的公司时，就会触发“平衡调整事件”。简单来说，汽车的任何收益或损失都会影响她的个人应税收入。

这意味着任何收益或损失都可能影响她的个人应税收入 - 但如果她满足某些条件，可能有小型企业重组展期（Subdivision328-G）可以帮助她避免立即纳税。

实际上，大多数商务车都会贬值，因此任何收益或损失通常都很小，而且展期很少能提供显着的税收优惠。

艾玛还需要转让注册并缴纳印花税，这样公司就成为合法所有者。

在你决定之前——问问自己

• 我预计未来几年的利润是多少？

• 我个人愿意承担多大的风险？

• 我会将利润再投资还是分配？

• 我打算雇用员工或吸引投资者吗？

• 我需要资产保护或额外的信誉吗？

• 我可以接受更多的文书工作以获得更好的长期利益吗？

关于个人服务收入 (PSI) 的简要说明

如果您的大部分收入来自您的个人技能或劳动（例如咨询或自由职业），PSI 规则可能适用。它们会影响您申请扣除的方式以及是否允许通过公司进行收入分配。我们有一个单独的博客，用简单的英语解释 PSI。

底线

个体经营者：简单、灵活、成本低——非常适合起步。
公司：结构化、保护性和可扩展性——非常适合成长。

许多澳大利亚企业都是从个体经营者开始的，并在需要升级时转向公司。

要点：选择与目标、收入和风险承受能力匹配的结构，并在执行前让会计师复核。

有疑问吗？通过应用程序聊天联系我们。您还可以在此网站上找到有关业务结构的更多信息。

title: EOFY Survival Guide for Australian Small Businesses
title_zh: 澳大利亚小型企业/公司的 EOFY 生存指南
author: Livia Lin, CA CTA
excerpt: EOFY can be straightforward with the right process. Our survival guide is designed to help Aussie small businesseses/companies tackle tax, BAS, and compliance without the headache, so you may wrap things up with confidence
excerpt_zh: EOFY 不一定很可怕。我们的生存指南可帮助澳大利亚小型企业/公司轻松解决税务、商业运作准则和合规问题，因此您可以充满信心地解决问题
content_text:
Editor note: The following article has been adapted for Flexpath readers while preserving the same technical meaning.

EOFY doesn’t have to be scary. Our survival guide helps Aussie small businesses/companies tackle tax, BAS, and compliance without the headache, so you may wrap things up with confidence

Overview at a Glance

• Always keep your books tidy: Keep financial records and bank reconciliations up to date

• Start early: May is the ideal time to start planning for tax and collating key documents

• Finalise payroll: Complete STP reporting and ensure all employee entitlements are paid on time

• Year-end adjustments: Post all adjusting journals to finalise your accounts for the financial year

• Lodge and pay on time: Review your business tax obligations to stay compliant

Why EOFY Matters for Small Companies

For directors and owners of Australian small proprietary companies, End of Financial Year (EOFY) is more than just lodging a tax return. It’s your annual opportunity to assess your business health, plan for cash flow and tax savings, and make sure you’re compliant with the ATO, ASIC, and other regulators. Yes, it’s a series of obligations, but it’s also a good time to take a snapshot of your company and make sure everything is heading in the right direction. So with that in mind, here’s your comprehensive guide to getting your business  EOFY-ready.

When is EOFY?

For most small businesses in Australia, the financial year ends on 30 June each year. Each financial year covers the period from 1 July to 30 June.

Why start early in May?

As with most things in life, it’s not a good idea to leave your EOFY to the last minute. 
May is the perfect time for small business owners to get ahead of EOFY. Starting early gives you time to organise records, identify deductions, review cash flow, and make strategic decisions before 30 June, without a panicky scramble before the deadline.
Accounts get busy! From March onwards, accountants enter the most hectic period, finalising prior-year lodgements, making it harder to get their time and attention - and if you wait until they contact you, you risk missing opportunities for tax savings, cash flow planning, and stress-free compliance.

Get your records in order

Before the admin piles up, take some time to get organised. Key tasks for small business owners include:

• Reconciling all business bank accounts, credit cards, loans, and petty cash

• Making sure income and expenses are correctly categorised and recorded

• Collating all your records for tax-sensitive items. These include:

• Legal feesHome office expensesAsset purchases or improvementsLoan agreements and repayment schedulesMaterial business expenses

• Verifying your accounts receivable (that’s money owed to you) and accounts payable (money you owe)

• Reviewing your  insurance policies

• Checking that your ATO and ASIC obligations are up to date, including:

• Outstanding BAS/IAS, income tax, FBT, PAYG instalmentsOutstanding super paymentsOutstanding ASIC fees

• Renewing business name registrations

• Reviewing company details, and storing constitution and governance documents

Planning before 30 June

Good planning before EOFY can help reduce your tax liability (that’s how much tax you owe) and improve your cash flow. By reviewing your business activities and financial position early, you may take advantage of available concessions, maximise the deductions, and set yourself up for a smoother year ahead.

Have a think about these strategies before 30 June:

• Do a stocktake and write off obsolete or slow-moving inventory

• Write off any bad debts

• Delay issuing invoices if it benefits your tax position (deferring income to next year)

• Review and apply small business tax concessions

• Prepay expenses such as superannuation, rent, insurance, and subscriptions

• Take advantage of the instant asset write-off for eligible assets purchased and installed by 30 June

• Prepare budgets and forecast your tax position to plan cash flow and payments

• Review Director Loan balances and Division 7A risks

• Evaluate your business structure and ask for advice if you need it

• Set aside money for income tax, June BAS, super, and other tax obligations

• Ask for Quantity Surveyors (depreciation report) for business premises refurbishment if applicable

• Talk to experts if you’re unsure of anything

Actions after 30 June

Once the financial year closes, finalise all pending tasks to make sure you’re compliant and a have a smooth, straightforward transition into the new year. You should also:

• Collect together all your year-end documents, including:

• Bank and credit card statementsYear-end tax summaries for investmentsLoan statements with updated balances

• Finalise payroll processing and lodge STP reports by 14 July

• Lodge and pay the June BAS and super by 28 July

• Record year-end adjustments, including but not limited to:

• Division 7A prepayments, accruals, provisionsTrading stock adjustmentsDepreciation and amortisationHire purchase adjustmentsAdjusting entries due to prior-year timing difference

• Review major sales and purchases

• Prepare a Profit & Loss statement and compare results with last year

• Reconcile Balance Sheet items and make sure supporting documents are on file

• Reconcile ATO tax liabilities, including GST, PAYGW, and income tax

• Review Director Loan accounts

• Assess available tax incentives and eligibility, such as:

• Prior year lossesAvailable franking creditsInstant asset write-offR&D tax incentivesCapital gains/lossesEntertainment and donationsLate super paymentsBad debtsFines and penaltiesCapital expenses

• Know your due dates and lodge your company income tax return, taxable payments annual report (TPAR) for certain industries

• Finalise budgets, cash flow forecasts, and business plans for the new financial year

Final thoughts

With the right process and tools, EOFY can become manageable and far less stressful. Because you started a business to grow, not to do your taxes!

Disclaimer:
This content is general information only and does not constitute financial, accounting, or tax advice. Seek professional advice tailored to your circumstances before making decisions.
content_text_zh:
EOFY 不一定很可怕。我们的生存指南可帮助澳大利亚小型企业/公司轻松解决税务、商业运作准则和合规问题，因此您可以充满信心地解决问题

快速总结

• 始终保持账簿整洁：保持最新的财务记录和银行对账单

• 尽早开始：五月是开始规划税务和整理关键文件的理想时间

• 最终确定工资单：完成 STP 报告并确保按时支付所有员工权利

• 年终调整：过帐所有调整日记帐以最终确定本财政年度的账目

• 按时提交并付款：审查您的营业税义务以保持合规

为什么 EOFY 对小公司很重要

对于澳大利亚小型专有公司的董事和所有者来说，财政年度结束（EOFY）不仅仅是提交纳税申报表。您每年都有机会评估您的业务健康状况、规划现金流和节税，并确保您遵守 ATO、ASIC 和其他监管机构的规定。是的，这是一系列义务，但也是一个了解公司概况并确保一切都朝着正确方向发展的好时机。 因此，考虑到这一点，这里是让您的企业为 EOFY 做好准备的综合指南。

EOFY 是什么时候？

对于澳大利亚的大多数小型企业来说，财政年度于每年 6 月 30 日结束。每个财政年度涵盖7月1日至6月30日期间。

为什么要在五月初开始？

与生活中的大多数事情一样，把你的 EOFY 留到最后一刻并不是一个好主意。 
五月是小企业主提前度过 EOFY 的最佳时机。提早开始可以让您有时间在 6 月 30 日之前整理记录、确定扣除额、审查现金流并做出战略决策，而不必在截止日期前惊慌失措。
账户开始忙碌！从三月份开始，会计师进入最忙碌的时期，完成上一年的申报，这使得他们更难获得他们的时间和关注——如果你等到他们联系你，你就有可能错过节税、现金流规划和无压力合规的机会。

按顺序获取您的记录

在管理工作堆积起来之前，花一些时间来组织一下。小企业主的主要任务包括：

• 核对所有商业银行账户、信用卡、贷款和零用现金

• 确保收入和支出正确分类和记录

• 整理税务敏感项目的所有记录。这些包括：

• 法律费用家庭办公费用资产购买或改良贷款协议和还款计划重大业务费用

• 验证您的应收账款（即欠您的钱）和应付账款（您欠的钱）

• 审查您的保险单

• 检查您的 ATO 和 ASIC 义务是否是最新的，包括：

• 未付的 BAS/IAS、所得税、FBT、PAYG 分期付款 未付的超级付款 未付的 ASIC 费用

• 更新企业名称注册

• 审查公司详细信息，并存储章程和治理文件

6月30日前计划

在 EOFY 之前做好规划可以帮助减少您的纳税义务（即您所欠的税款）并改善您的现金流。通过尽早审查您的业务活动和财务状况，您可以利用可用的优惠，最大限度地提高扣除额，并为未来的一年更加顺利做好准备。

在 6 月 30 日之前考虑一下这些策略：

• 进行盘点并注销过时或滞销的库存

• 冲销任何坏账

• 如果有利于您的税务状况，则延迟开具发票（将收入推迟到明年）

• 审查并应用小企业税收优惠

• 预付养老金、租金、保险和订阅费等费用

• 利用 6 月 30 日之前购买和安装的合格资产的即时资产核销

• 准备预算并预测您的税务状况以规划现金流和付款

• 审查总监贷款余额和第 7A 部门风险

• 评估您的业务结构并在需要时寻求建议

• 留出资金用于缴纳所得税、6 月 BAS、养老金和其他纳税义务

• 要求工料测量师（折旧报告）进行营业场所翻新（如果适用）

• 如果您有任何疑问，请咨询专家

6 月 30 日后的行动

财政年度结束后，完成所有待处理的任务，以确保您合规并顺利、直接地过渡到新的一年。您还应该：

• 收集所有年终文件，包括：

• 银行和信用卡对账单投资的年终税务摘要带有更新余额的贷款对账单

• 在 7 月 14 日之前完成薪资处理并提交 STP 报告

• 在 7 月 28 日之前提交并支付 6 月 BAS 和 Super

• 记录年终调整，包括但不限于：

• 第 7A 部门预付款项、应计费用、准备金 交易库存调整 折旧和摊销 租购调整 由于上一年时间差异而调整分录

• 审查主要销售和采购

• 准备损益表并将结果与去年进行比较

• 核对资产负债表项目并确保支持文件存档

• 协调 ATO 税务负债，包括 GST、PAYGW 和所得税

• 审查董事贷款账户

• 评估可用的税收优惠和资格，例如：

• 上一年亏损 可用红利抵免 即时资产冲销 研发税收优惠 资本利得/损失 娱乐和捐赠 逾期超级付款 坏账 罚款和处罚 资本支出

• 了解您的截止日期并提交您的公司所得税申报表、某些行业的应税付款年度报告（TPAR）

• 最终确定新财政年度的预算、现金流量预测和业务计划

最后的想法

通过前置规划与工具化流程，EOFY 完全可以从“压力项”变成“管理项”。

免责声明：
本内容仅供一般信息参考，不构成财务、会计或税务建议；在作出决定前请咨询专业人士。

title: ACN vs ABN vs TFN: What Each One Means
title_zh: ACN、ABN、TFN：它们是什么以及您真的需要它们吗？
author: Livia Lin, CA CTA
excerpt: Starting a business in Australia? You’ve probably heard these three sets of  letters thrown around: ACN, ABN, TFN. They might sound similar, but each has a different purpose. in this article will help you understand what they are, who issues them, whether you need them, and how they differ
excerpt_zh: 在澳大利亚创业？您可能听说过这三组字母：ACN、ABN、TFN。它们可能听起来很相似，但每个都有不同的目的。本指南将帮助您了解它们是什么、谁发布它们、您是否需要它们以及它们有何不同
content_text:
Editor note: The following article has been adapted for Flexpath readers while preserving the same technical meaning.

Starting a business in Australia? You’ve probably heard these three sets of  letters thrown around: ACN, ABN, TFN. They might sound similar, but each has a different purpose. This article will help you understand what they are, who issues them, whether you need them, and how they differ

Overview at a Glance

What’s an ACN?

ACN stands for Australian Company Number. It’s a unique 9-digit unique number issued to every company registered in Australia.

Who issues it?

The Australian Securities and Investments Commission (ASIC) issues ACNs when a company is registered.

What’s it for?

Identifies your company on the ASIC register.

Used on official company documents and legal paperwork.

Helps distinguish your company from others with similar names.

Do you need it?

Only if you are registering a company. Sole traders, trusts and partnerships don’t get an ACN.

Example:

ACN: 123 456 789

Think of ACN as your company’s “ID card” with ASIC.

What is an ABN?

ABN stands for Australian Business Number. It’s a unique 11-digit number used for business dealings with the government and other businesses.

Who issues it?

The Australian Taxation Office (ATO) issues ABNs through the business registration process.

What’s it for?

Identifies your business for tax purposes.

Allows you to register for GST, PAYG withholding, and other business taxes.

Needed for invoicing, claiming GST credits, and dealing with other businesses.

Do you need it?

Yes, if you’re running a business, regardless of the structure - a sole trader, partnership, company, or trust - you need an ABN.

Example:

ABN: 12 345 678 901

ABN is like your business “passport” for dealing with other businesses and the government.

What is a TFN?

TFN stands for Tax File Number. It is a unique 9-digit number issued to individuals, companies, partnerships, trusts, and superannuation funds for tax purposes.

Who issues it?

The Australian Taxation Office (ATO) issues ABNs through the business registration process.

What’s it for?

Used for income tax and other tax reporting.

You need it to open bank accounts, apply for government benefits, and lodge tax returns.

Do you need it?

Yes. Every person and entity earning income or operating a business in Australia must have a TFN.

Example:

TFN: 123 456 789

TFN is your personal or business “tax ID.”

Disclaimer:
This content is general information only and does not constitute financial, accounting, or tax advice. Seek professional advice tailored to your circumstances before making decisions.
content_text_zh:
在澳大利亚创业？您可能听说过这三组字母：ACN、ABN、TFN。它们可能听起来很相似，但每个都有不同的目的。本指南将帮助您了解它们是什么、谁发布它们、您是否需要它们以及它们有何不同

快速总结

什么是 ACN？

ACN 代表澳大利亚公司编号。这是向每家在澳大利亚注册的公司颁发的唯一 9 位数字。

谁颁发的？

公司注册时，澳大利亚证券和投资委员会 (ASIC) 会颁发 ACN。

这是做什么用的？

在 ASIC 登记册上标识您的公司。

用于正式公司文件和法律文书。

有助于将您的公司与名称相似的其他公司区分开来。

你需要吗？

仅当您注册公司时。个体经营者、信托公司和合伙企业没有 ACN。

示例：

丙烯腈: 123 456 789

将 ACN 视为您公司带有 ASIC 的“身份证”。

什么是 ABN？

ABN 代表澳大利亚商业号码。这是一个独特的 11 位数字，用于与政府和其他企业进行业务往来。

谁颁发的？

澳大利亚税务局 (ATO) 通过企业注册流程颁发 ABN。

这是做什么用的？

出于税务目的识别您的企业。

允许您注册 GST、现收现付预扣税和其他营业税。

需要开发票、申请商品及服务税抵免以及处理其他业务。

你需要吗？

是的，如果您经营一家企业，无论其结构如何 - 个体经营者、合伙企业、公司或信托 - 您都需要 ABN。

示例：

澳大利亚号码：12 345 678 901

ABN 就像您与其他企业和政府打交道的企业“护照”。

什么是税号？

TFN 代表税号。它是一个独特的 9 位数号码，出于税务目的向个人、公司、合伙企业、信托和养老基金发放。

谁颁发的？

澳大利亚税务局 (ATO) 通过企业注册流程颁发 ABN。

这是做什么用的？

用于所得税和其他税务报告。

您需要它来开设银行账户、申请政府福利和提交纳税申报表。

你需要吗？

是的。每个在澳大利亚赚取收入或经营业务的个人和实体都必须拥有 TFN。

示例：

税号：123 456 789

TFN 是您的个人或企业“税号”。

免责声明：
本内容仅供一般信息参考，不构成财务、会计或税务建议；在作出决定前请咨询专业人士。

title: Company Income Tax in Australia: Quick Start Guide
author: Livia Lin, CA CTA
excerpt: A simple guide to company income tax in Australia. Learn tax rates, lodgement rules, and key tips to manage your business tax obligations effectively
excerpt_zh: 澳大利亚公司所得税的简单指南。了解税率、申报规则和有效管理营业税义务的关键提示
content_text:
Editor note: The following article has been adapted for Flexpath readers while preserving the same technical meaning.

A practical primer on company income tax in Australia. Learn tax rates, lodgement rules, and key tips to manage your business tax obligations effectively

Overview at a Glance:

• Company tax rate: 25% on net profits for most small company businesses

• Company Income Tax due dates: The due date may vary. Generally, 28 Feb (self-prepared with no pass return outstanding) or up to 15 May with a tax agent

• How to lodge: Submit your company income tax return via a SBR-enabled software, or through your accountant

• Penalties: Late tax returns can mean fines from $330 plus daily compounding interest

What is Company Income Tax?

It’s the tax your company pays on its business profits - that means the total income minus your business expenses. For the 2025 financial year (1 July 2025 to 30 June 2026), the Company Income Tax rate is 25% for small businesses. 

Every company in Australia needs to lodge a Company Income Tax Return once a year.

What goes in a Company Income Tax Return?

Here are the key things you’ll need to include in your Company Income Tax Return:

• Total income: Such as sales, services, and any other money coming in

• Business expenses: Such as rent, wages, subscriptions, the usual suspects

• Any losses from previous years: Sometimes previous losses can be used to offset the profit your business has made in current period profit. So include any previous tax losses, they may help you reduce the tax you’ll have to pay

• Tax instalments already paid: Tax instalments are pre-paid taxes that ATO may need in certain circumstances. When you’re filling in your final year end assessment, make sure to include the taxes you’ve already paid so you’re not taxed twice

Need a clearer picture? See a sample return for FY 2025.

When is Company Income Tax due?

Generally, the lodgement and payment date for small companies is 28 February if you lodge it yourself, rather than using a tax agent. If you have any previous year returns still outstanding, the due date will be 31 October. If you’re using a tax agent or ask for a deferral, you might get a bit more time to file your Return.

Quick Tips:

• Keep it Clean No cents. No rounding up. Whole dollars only on Income Tax.

• Nothing to report? Lodge a NIL Income Tax Return through your usual lodgement channel.

• Oops! Made a mistake? Lodge a Company Income Tax Amendment to fix the error. However, you may incur interest and penalties if the amendment increases the money you owe.

• Stay on top of it: keep solid records of sales and expenses, categorise transactions consistently, and maintain supporting evidence.

• Stash tax cash as you go: set aside estimated tax regularly to avoid year-end surprises.

Got questions? Reach out to us in the app chat. You can also find more info about Income Tax on the ATO website.

Disclaimer:
This content is general information only and does not constitute financial, accounting, or tax advice. Seek professional advice tailored to your circumstances before making decisions.
content_text_zh:
澳大利亚公司所得税的简单指南。了解税率、申报规则和有效管理营业税义务的关键提示

快速总结：

• 公司税率：大多数小型公司的净利润为 25%

• 公司所得税到期日：到期日可能有所不同。一般情况下，2 月 28 日（自行准备且无未完成的通行证申报表）或由税务代理人在 5 月 15 日之前

• 如何提交：通过支持 SBR 的软件或通过您的会计师提交您的公司所得税申报表

• 处罚：逾期纳税申报可能意味着 330 美元的罚款加上每日复利

什么是公司所得税？

这是您的公司为其业务利润缴纳的税款 - 这意味着总收入减去您的业务费用。 2025财年（2025年7月1日至2026年6月30日），小型企业的公司所得税率为25%。 

澳大利亚的每家公司都需要每年提交一次公司所得税申报表。

公司所得税申报表中包含哪些内容？

以下是您需要在公司所得税申报表中包含的关键内容：

• 总收入：例如销售、服务和任何其他收入

• 业务开支：例如租金、工资、订阅费、常见费用

• 前几年的任何损失：有时，以前的损失可以用来抵消您的企业在当期利润中获得的利润。因此，包括之前的任何税收损失，它们可能会帮助您减少需要缴纳的税款

• 已缴税款：税款分期付款是ATO 在某些情况下可能需要的预缴税款。当您填写最终年终评估时，请确保包含您已缴纳的税款，这样您就不会被重复纳税

需要更清晰的图片吗？请参阅 2025 财年的申报表样本。

公司所得税何时缴纳？

一般来说，如果您自己提交，而不是通过税务代理人提交，小公司的提交和付款日期是 2 月 28 日。如果您还有任何上一年的申报表尚未结清，截止日期将为 10 月 31 日。如果您使用税务代理或要求延期，您可能会有更多时间来提交纳税申报表。

快速提示：

• 保持清洁不花一分钱。没有四舍五入。仅缴纳所得税。

• 没有可申报内容？可通过常用申报渠道提交零申报。

• 哎呀！犯错了吗？ 提交公司所得税修正案以纠正错误。但是，如果修正案增加了您所欠的金额，您可能会产生利息和罚款。

• 掌握情况：保持销售与费用记录完整、分类一致，并留存支持证据。

• 预留税款现金：按周期预估并预留税款，减少年末资金压力。

免责声明：
本内容仅供一般信息参考，不构成财务、会计或税务建议；在作出决定前请咨询专业人士。

title: GST and BAS in Australia: Simple Beginner Guide
author: Livia Lin, CA CTA
excerpt: Understand GST and BAS in Australia with this practical guide. Learn how GST works, when to register, and how to lodge BAS to stay compliant with the ATO
excerpt_zh: 通过本快速指南了解澳大利亚的 GST 和 BAS。了解 GST 如何运作、何时注册以及如何提交 BAS 以遵守 ATO
content_text:
Editor note: The following article has been adapted for Flexpath readers while preserving the same technical meaning.

Understand GST and BAS in Australia with this practical overview. Learn how GST works, when to register, and how to lodge BAS to stay compliant with the ATO

Overview at a Glance:

• GST registration: Required if your annual GST turnover is $75,000 or more

• BAS due dates: Most small businesses lodge BAS quarterly, due 28 Oct, 28 Feb, 28 Apr, 28 Jul

• How to lodge: Submit your BAS online, via your ATO online services, a SBR-enabled software, or through your accountant

• Penalties: Late BAS lodgement can mean fines from $330 plus daily compounding interest

What is GST?

GST (Goods and Services Tax) is a 10% tax on most goods and services sold in Australia. If you're registered for GST, you're expected to charge it on sales, pay it to the ATO, and claim back the GST on your eligible business purchases.

Who needs to register for GST?

If needed, use a registered tax agent or trusted accounting platform to help with GST registration.

What is a BAS?

A Business Activity Statement (BAS) is your regular tax check-in with the ATO. It’s where you report and pay GST, PAYG withholding (tax on wages), PAYG instalments (prepaid tax), and other business taxes. Most small businesses lodge it quarterly. See a sample.

What’s an IAS?

An Instalment Activity Statement (IAS) is like a mini BAS. It’s used to report and pay things like PAYG instalments or withholding tax. You’ll usually get one monthly from the ATO if you should lodge it. See a sample.

So, what’s the difference between BAS and IAS?

Both are ATO forms used to report and pay business taxes but they serve different setups:

BAS is the full kit: GST, PAYG withholding, PAYG instalments, and more
IAS is the smaller version: no GST, just PAYG obligations.

Example Time:
Let’s say your business is registered for GST and lodges BAS quarterly. You’ve also got employees, so you’re withholding PAYG tax from their wages. If your payroll hits a certain threshold, the ATO might ask you to report PAYG withholding monthly, even if everything else stays quarterly. In that case, you’ll lodge a quarterly BAS for GST and PAYG instalments, and a monthly IAS just to handle PAYG withholding for your team.

Still Confused? Check out ‘Simpler BAS’
If your GST turnover is under $10 million, you only need to report 3 things under the “Simpler BAS” method:
 G1 – Total Sales
 1A – GST on Sales
 1B – GST on Purchases

Great, so when is BAS due?

If you're on quarterly BAS, your due dates are below:

On monthly BAS? Pop into the app chat and give us a wave, we'll help you out.

Bonus: Lodging online? You could get up to 2 extra weeks, except for Q2 (1 Oct to 31 Dec), as the due date of 28 Feb already includes a Christmas extension.

Use calendar reminders so lodgement and payment due dates are not missed.

Quick Tips:

• Keep it Clean: No cents. No rounding up. Whole dollars only on IAS/BAS.

• Nothing to report? Lodge a NIL IAS/BAS through your normal lodgement channel.

• Oops! Made a mistake? Fix it in your next BAS or lodge a revision. Use code 1A/1B to correct GST on sales/purchases.

• PAYG Instalment got you panicking? Too high? Just vary it (code T9, reason code T4). Overpaid last time? Claim a credit on B5.
Heads up: If your instalments are too low, the ATO may charge interest.

• Stay on top of it: keep accurate transaction matching and supporting evidence so BAS preparation is straightforward.

• Stash tax cash as you go: set aside estimated tax regularly to avoid year-end surprises.

Got questions? Reach out to us in the app chat. You can also find more info about GST/BAS on the ATO website.

Disclaimer:
This content is general information only and does not constitute financial, accounting, or tax advice. Seek professional advice tailored to your circumstances before making decisions.

通过本快速指南了解澳大利亚的 GST 和 BAS。了解 GST 如何运作、何时注册以及如何提交 BAS 以遵守 ATO

快速总结：

• GST 注册：如果您的 GST 年营业额达到或超过 75,000 美元，则需要注册

• BAS 截止日期：大多数小型企业按季度提交 BAS，截止日期为 10 月 28 日、2 月 28 日、4 月 28 日、7 月 28 日

• 如何提交：通过 ATO 在线服务、支持 SBR 的软件或通过您的会计师在线提交您的 BAS

• 处罚：逾期提交 BAS 可能意味着 330 美元的罚款外加每日复利

什么是商品及服务税？

GST（商品及服务税）是对在澳大利亚销售的大多数商品和服务征收 10% 的税。如果您注册了商品及服务税，则需要对销售收取费用，向 ATO 支付，并针对符合条件的商业采购申请退还商品及服务税。

谁需要注册消费税？

什么是BAS？

商业活动报表 (BAS) 是您向 ATO 进行的常规税务登记。您可以在此处申报和支付商品及服务税、现收现付预扣税（工资税）、现收现付分期付款（预付税）和其他营业税。大多数小型企业每季度提交一次。查看示例。

什么是 IAS？

分期付款活动报表 (IAS) 就像迷你 BAS。它用于报告和支付现收现付分期付款或预扣税等费用。如果您需要提交，通常会每月从 ATO 收到一张。查看示例。

那么，BAS和IAS有什么区别呢？

两者都是用于报告和缴纳营业税的 ATO 表格，但它们提供不同的设置：

BAS 是完整的套件：GST、现收现付预扣税、现收现付分期付款等
IAS 是较小的版本：没有消费税，只有现收现付义务。

示例时间：
假设您的企业已注册商品及服务税 (GST) 并按季度提交 BAS。您还有员工，因此您从他们的工资中预扣现收现付税。如果您的工资达到一定阈值，ATO 可能会要求您每月报告现收现付预扣税，即使其他一切都按季度报告。在这种情况下，您将按季度提交 GST 和 PAYG 分期付款的 BAS，并每月提交 IAS，以便为您的团队处理 PAYG 预扣税。

仍然困惑吗？查看“更简单的 BAS”
如果您的 GST 营业额低于 1000 万澳元，您只需按照“更简单的 BAS”方法报告 3 件事：
 G1 – 总销售额
 1A – 销售商品及服务税
 1B – 购买商品及服务税

太好了，那么 BAS 什么时候到期？

如果您使用的是季度 BAS，您的截止日期如下：

每月 BAS 吗？进入应用程序聊天并向我们挥手，我们将为您提供帮助。

奖励：网上住宿？除第二季度（10 月 1 日至 12 月 31 日）外，您最多可以获得额外 2 周的延期，因为 2 月 28 日的截止日期已经包括圣诞节延期。

可设置日历提醒，避免错过申报与付款截止日。

快速提示：

• 保持清洁：不花一分钱。没有四舍五入。仅针对 IAS/BAS 的整美元。

• 没有可申报内容？可通过常用渠道提交 NIL IAS/BAS。

• 哎呀！犯错了吗？在您的下一个 BAS 中修复它或提交修订。使用代码 1A/1B 更正销售/购买的 GST。

• PAYG 分期付款让您惊慌失措？太高了？只需改变它（代码 T9，原因代码 T4）。上次多付钱了？在 B5 上申请积分。
请注意：如果您的分期付款太低，ATO 可能会收取利息。

• 掌控全局：做好交易匹配并留存证据，可显著提升 BAS 编制效率。

• 预留 GST 资金：按收入比例定期转入税款备用账户，减少到期付款压力。
